title: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
one_line_summary: The JCS 2026 Guideline addresses the rising incidence and complexity of infective endocarditis (IE), emphasizing a multidisciplinary approach for diagnosis, antimicrobial therapy, surgical indications, and prevention strategies.
key_pearls: For suspected IE, obtain 3 blood cultures before antibiotics; perform TTE within 24h, escalating to TEE for prosthetic valves, devices, or inconclusive TTE.
Initiate empiric antibiotics based on valve type and MRSA risk; use therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) for vancomycin, teicoplanin, and aminoglycosides.
Daily cardiac auscultation and ECG are crucial to detect new murmurs or PR prolongation, signaling perivalvular infection and need for urgent TEE/surgical evaluation.

Emergency surgery (within 24h) is indicated for cardiogenic shock or refractory pulmonary edema; urgent surgery for persistent bacteremia >7 days, perivalvular abscess, or vegetation >10mm with prior embolism.
Antibiotic prophylaxis is strongly recommended for high-risk patients (e.g., prosthetic valves, prior IE) undergoing invasive dental procedures (Amoxicillin 2g PO 1hr pre-procedure).
For IE complicated by cerebral hemorrhage, surgery can proceed within 4 weeks if urgent indication and neurological stability; however, delay for extensive stroke or expanding hemorrhage.
Complete removal of the entire system (generator and leads) is the cornerstone of treatment for CIED infections, followed by 2-6 weeks of targeted antibiotics.
consensus_method: GRADE (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation) and modified Delphi (RAND) method

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Infective endocarditis (IE) diagnosis formally adopts the updated Duke-ISCVID 2023 criteria, which refine the previous 2015 modified Duke criteria by incorporating new imaging modalities and expanding microbiological criteria. Pathological criteria for definite diagnosis include microorganism identification (culture, PCR, sequencing) or histological evidence of active endocarditis in cardiac tissues or emboli. Clinical criteria define definite IE as 2 major, or 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor criteria. Major criteria include positive blood cultures for typical IE pathogens (e.g., *S. aureus*, *Enterococcus faecalis*, Viridans group streptococci, HACEK group) or specific intracellular pathogens (*Coxiella burnetii*, *Bartonella* spp.), and imaging evidence (echocardiogram/cardiac CT showing vegetation, abscess, new regurgitation; or 18F-FDG PET/CT activity on prosthetic valves >3 months post-implant). Minor criteria include predisposition, fever >38.0°C, vascular phenomena (systemic emboli, mycotic aneurysm), immunological phenomena (Osler's nodes, Roth spots, glomerulonephritis), additional microbiological evidence, or additional imaging findings (PET/CT activity <3 months post-implant).

recommendations.statement: Utilize the Duke-ISCVID 2023 criteria for definitive and possible diagnosis of infective endocarditis, integrating advanced imaging and expanded microbiological testing.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Heart failure (HF) is the most common and critical complication of left-sided IE (approx. 40% of native valve cases), primarily due to acute valvular destruction, leading to high short- and medium-term mortality. Uncontrolled infection is defined by persistent fever/positive blood cultures after 7 days of adequate antibiotics, or development of perivalvular abscess, fistula, or pseudoaneurysm, more common in prosthetic valve IE. Neurological events occur in 20-50% of IE patients, with CNS involvement in 60-70% of embolic events, including ischemic stroke, intracranial hemorrhage (ICH), and cerebral abscess. MRI with DWI/SWI is highly sensitive for subclinical microemboli. Vegetations >10mm, especially mobile mitral valve vegetations, confer the highest embolic risk. Other systemic complications include acute kidney injury (53-69% of patients), disseminated intravascular coagulation (1-32%), and pyogenic spondylitis (5-10%).

recommendations.statement: Actively screen for and manage complications such as heart failure, perivalvular abscess, and neurological events, which significantly impact IE prognosis.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Antimicrobial Therapy Principles and Specific Pathogen Regimens
recommendation_blocks.narrative: Antimicrobial therapy for IE must be bactericidal, administered intravenously at high doses. At least 3 sets of blood cultures must be collected before starting antibiotics. The 'Day 0' for treatment duration is the day blood cultures become negative. Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is strongly recommended for vancomycin (target AUC/MIC 400-600 or trough 15-20 mcg/mL), teicoplanin (trough ≥ 20 mcg/mL), and aminoglycosides (gentamicin peak 10-12 mcg/mL for QD, trough <1 mcg/mL) to optimize efficacy and minimize nephrotoxicity. Empirical regimens vary by valve type and MRSA risk. Specific regimens are detailed for *Streptococcus* spp. (e.g., Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone for 4-6 weeks, 2-week short course with Gentamicin for native valve if specific criteria met), *Staphylococcus* spp. (Cefazolin for MSSA, Vancomycin/Daptomicin for MRSA), *Enterococcus* spp. (Ampicillin + Gentamicin or Ampicillin + Ceftriaxone), HACEK group (Ceftriaxone), and Fungi (Liposomal Amphotericin B + echinocandin, often requiring surgery and long-term oral azole suppression).

recommendations.statement: Initiate empiric bactericidal intravenous antibiotic therapy promptly after collecting blood cultures, adjusting based on culture results and pathogen sensitivities.
Perform therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) for vancomycin, teicoplanin, and aminoglycosides to ensure optimal drug levels and minimize toxicity.

Follow specific pathogen-directed regimens and durations (e.g., 4-6 weeks for native valve, 6-8 weeks for prosthetic valve) as outlined in the guideline, considering combination therapy where indicated.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Surgical Treatment: Risk Assessment, Indications, and Timing
recommendation_blocks.narrative: Surgical intervention in active IE is complex, with traditional risk scores underestimating mortality due to lack of infection-specific variables. A multidisciplinary 'Infective Endocarditis Team' discussion is recommended for each case. Key surgical indications include heart failure (emergency for cardiogenic shock/refractory pulmonary edema; urgency for NYHA Class III/IV HF), uncontrolled infection (perivalvular abscess, fistula, new conduction disturbances, persistent sepsis after 3-5 days of antibiotics, or fungal/highly resistant organisms), and prevention of embolism (persistent vegetation >10mm after embolic events, or mobile vegetation >10mm with progressive valvular dysfunction). For IE with neurological complications, timing is critical: early surgery is recommended for ischemic stroke without severe deficit, but delayed for 1-2 weeks for extensive stroke. For intracranial hemorrhage, surgery can be performed within 2 weeks if urgent indication and neurological stability, but delayed for at least 4 weeks if expanding hematoma. Mycotic aneurysms >5mm should be treated before urgent cardiac surgery.

recommendations.statement: Perform emergency cardiac surgery (within 24 hours) for cardiogenic shock or refractory pulmonary edema caused by severe valvular regurgitation or intracardiac fistula.

Perform urgent cardiac surgery (within days) for NYHA Class III/IV heart failure, perivalvular abscess, fistula, pseudoaneurysm, increasing vegetation size under antibiotics, new conduction disturbances, or persistent sepsis/septic shock after 3-5 days of adequate antibiotics.

Perform urgent cardiac surgery for persistent vegetation >10mm after one or more systemic embolic episodes under antibiotic therapy, or for mobile vegetation >10mm associated with progressive valvular dysfunction.
Consider surgery for isolated mobile vegetation >10mm in the absence of other surgical indications, provided surgical risk is low (Class IIb).

For IE complicated by ischemic stroke without severe neurological deficit, early cardiac surgery is recommended if there is a formal indication. For extensive stroke or severe deficit (NIHSS >11), delay surgery for at least 1-2 weeks.

For IE complicated by cerebral hemorrhage, cardiac surgery can be performed within 4 weeks if there is an urgent surgical indication and confirmed neurological stability, but delay for at least 4 weeks if expanding hematoma or neurological instability.
Treat intracranial mycotic aneurysms >5mm (endovascular or neurosurgical) prior to urgent cardiac surgery.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: The JCS 2026 guideline maintains a comprehensive approach to antibiotic prophylaxis, recommending it for high-risk patients and considering it for moderate-risk patients, a stance that partially diverges from some Western guidelines. High-risk patients include those with prosthetic valves, prior IE, unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease, repaired congenital heart disease with prosthetic material within 6 months, or persistent residual defects. Moderate-risk patients include those with acquired valvulopathy, bicuspid aortic valve, mitral valve prolapse with regurgitation, hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, CIEDs, or heart transplant recipients with valvulopathy. Prophylaxis is recommended for invasive dental procedures involving manipulation of gingival tissue, periapical region, or oral mucosa perforation. Regimens include Amoxicillin 2g (adults) orally 1 hour before the procedure, or Clindamycin 600mg/Azithromycin 500mg/Clarithromycin 400mg for beta-lactam allergy. Intravenous options are Ampicillin 1-2g, Cefazolin 1g, or Ceftriaxone 1g, 30 minutes before.

recommendations.statement: Strongly recommend antibiotic prophylaxis before invasive dental procedures for patients at high risk of developing IE (e.g., prosthetic valves, prior IE, certain congenital heart diseases).
Suggest considering antibiotic prophylaxis before invasive dental procedures for patients at moderate risk of developing IE (e.g., acquired valvulopathy, bicuspid aortic valve, CIEDs).
Administer Amoxicillin 2g orally 1 hour before invasive dental procedures; for beta-lactam allergy, use Clindamycin 600mg or Azithromycin 500mg/Clarithromycin 400mg.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Special Conditions: Pediatrics, Devices, Right-Sided IE, and Pregnancy
recommendation_blocks.narrative: Pediatric IE is rare but primarily affects children with congenital heart disease; *S. aureus* is the most common pathogen. Surgical treatment in children prioritizes valvular preservation. For Cardiac Implantable Electronic Device (CIED) infections, the predominant pathogens are CoNS and *S. aureus*. Complete removal of the entire CIED system (generator and leads) is the cornerstone of treatment, ideally via percutaneous transvenous extraction, followed by 2-6 weeks of antibiotics. Right-sided IE accounts for 5-10% of cases, strongly associated with IV drug use and central venous catheters, primarily affecting the tricuspid valve. Prognosis is better than left-sided IE, with clinical cure rates around 90% with medical treatment alone. Surgery is indicated for large vegetations (>20mm) with recurrent pulmonary embolism, severe respiratory failure, acute RV dysfunction, or persistent sepsis. IE in pregnancy is extremely rare but carries high maternal and fetal mortality. Antibiotic selection must avoid teratogenicity (penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin are safe). Cardiac surgery during pregnancy should ideally be postponed or performed between 13-28 weeks if strictly necessary due to high fetal loss risk.

recommendations.statement: In pediatric IE, prioritize valvular preservation and repair over prosthetic valve replacement due to growth considerations.
For CIED infections, ensure complete removal of the entire system (generator and leads), preferably by percutaneous transvenous extraction, followed by appropriate antibiotic duration.

Consider surgery for right-sided IE in cases of large vegetations (>20mm) with recurrent pulmonary embolism, severe refractory respiratory failure, acute right ventricular dysfunction, or persistent sepsis.

Manage IE in pregnant patients with careful antibiotic selection (penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin are preferred) and defer cardiac surgery if possible, or perform between 13-28 weeks if essential.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Evidence for antibiotic prophylaxis (CQ1) showed a significant reduction in IE incidence for high-risk groups (OR 0.36; 95% CI: 0.23-0.56; p<0.001) in nested case-control studies. For moderate-risk groups, the reduction was not statistically significant (OR 1.29; 95% CI: 0.64-2.59; p=0.49), but temporal analyses (Dayer et al., 2015) suggested an increase in IE after prophylaxis cessation. Safety data for amoxicillin showed non-fatal adverse events at 21.4 per 1,000,000 doses (95% CI: 17.3-26.5) and fatal anaphylaxis at 0-0.11 per million doses. Clindamycin had higher fatal adverse events (12.6 per million doses, mainly associated with *Clostridioides difficile* colitis); non-fatal events at 149.1 per million doses. For early surgery post-ICH (CQ2), a meta-analysis of six observational studies (219 patients) found no significant difference in in-hospital mortality (RR 1.09; 95% CI: 0.46-2.56; p=0.84), rebleeding rate (RR 1.55; 95% CI: 0.72-3.30; p=0.26), or neurological deterioration (RR 1.91; 95% CI: 0.77-4.76; p=0.16) between early (<4 weeks) and delayed (>4 weeks) surgery. The panel concluded that early surgery is acceptable and often life-saving for refractory heart failure or uncontrolled infection, provided neurological stability and no expanding hematoma.

bedside_protocol.title: Immediate Diagnostic Investigation
bedside_protocol.action: Collect 3 sets of blood cultures (1 aerobic, 1 anaerobic per set) from distinct peripheral venipuncture sites. Perform Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE) within 24 hours; if negative or inadequate, or if prosthetic valve/device, perform Transesophageal Echocardiogram (TEE) immediately. Order cerebral MRI (DWI, FLAIR, SWI) for all patients with definite or possible left-sided IE, regardless of neurological symptoms, to screen for microemboli/microhemorrhages.

bedside_protocol.title: Antimicrobial Therapy Initiation and Monitoring
bedside_protocol.action: Start empiric antibiotics based on valvular status (native vs. prosthetic) and risk factors for MRSA. Perform Therapeutic Drug Monitoring (TDM) for vancomycin (target AUC/MIC 400-600 or trough 15-20 mcg/mL), teicoplanin (target trough ≥ 20 mcg/mL), and gentamicin (target peak 10-12 mcg/mL for QD, trough <1 mcg/mL). Monitor serum creatinine, electrolytes, and complete blood count (CBC) at least twice weekly during glycopeptide or aminoglycoside use.

bedside_protocol.title: Alarm Criteria and Surgical Triggers
bedside_protocol.action: Perform daily cardiac auscultation for new regurgitant murmurs (suggesting valvular destruction or fistula) and daily ECG or continuous monitoring for PR interval prolongation (suggesting perivalvular infection/interventricular septal abscess), which warrants urgent TEE and surgical evaluation. Activate surgical team for emergency (within 24h) if cardiogenic shock or refractory pulmonary edema. For persistent fever/bacteremia after 7 days of adequate antibiotics, perform a new TEE to rule out perivalvular abscess; if confirmed, schedule urgent surgery. For vegetation >10mm with a prior embolic event, schedule urgent surgery.

bedside_protocol.title: Transition to Outpatient Care (OPAT)
bedside_protocol.action: Consider Outpatient Parenteral Antimicrobial Therapy (OPAT) for clinically stable patients without decompensated heart failure, active surgical indication, recent neurological complications, or negative blood cultures, and with secure venous access (e.g., PICC line). Schedule a control echocardiogram at the end of treatment to establish a new valvular functional baseline. Maintain regular clinical follow-up, laboratory monitoring (CBC, CRP, creatinine), and rigorously educate the patient on oral hygiene and the necessity of lifelong antibiotic prophylaxis before invasive dental procedures.

strengths_limitations: The guideline was developed using the GRADE methodology and a modified Delphi (RAND) consensus process, involving structured clinical questions (PICO format), systematic literature searches, and anonymous expert evaluation to ensure evidence-based recommendations and consensus.
